text: What happens to the biologic prosthesis implanted during laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy? We present here intraoperative findings in two patients who previously underwent laparoscopic ventral rectopexy with biological mesh. In both cases we performed LVMR with cross-linked biologic mesh, acellular porcine dermal collagen, the distal end of the mesh was sutured to the interior rectal wall with a running absorbable three zero barbed suture and two additional non-absorbable two zero sticks of braided polyester the proximal end of the mesh was fixed to the sacral promontory with two non-absorbable two zero sticks of braided polyester and the peritoneum was closed using a three zero running absorbable barbed suture After LVMR, we usually follow up all the patients. In both cases, neither clinical nor radiological recurrence was registered.

During exploratory pelvic laparoscopy, the surgeon verifies the presence of the prosthetic mesh under the peritoneum in a 65-year-old female patient. The mesh exhibits suitable tension upon palpation with laparoscopic instruments, and there is no significant shrinkage observed.

The second patient was a sixty-four-year-old female who underwent laparoscopic incisional hernia repair. Twenty-three months before, she had been operated on for Grade IV rectal antisusception according to the Oxford Grading System. Our report may provide evidence demonstrating the in vivo durability of a biologic mesh after more than one year after its implant.
